final_answer: Assign each webhook a globally unique identifier (e.g., the provider's event_id) and store it together with a processing status in a dedicated idempotency table (N1:0:dc4855ad). Define a UNIQUE constraint on this idempotency_key (N2:1:c98e2afe) and use an upsert such as INSERT … ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING (N2:2:a9c1dfd2) so a duplicate receipt is ignored. When a webhook arrives, first attempt the insert; if it fails, the event has already been handled and you can return success without re‑applying business logic (N1:1:b4e4f3c9, N5:1:16d8f729). Wrap the insert of the idempotency record and the payment state change in a single SERIALIZABLE transaction (N2:3:47ad0397, N1:2:f7fb95d1) to guarantee atomicity and prevent partial updates. Make the payment update itself idempotent – e.g., increment only if the event has not been applied, using conditional upserts or version checks (N1:3:c3132779, N5:3:b45c76a2). Acknowledge the webhook to the sender immediately (HTTP 200/202) after persisting the idempotency record, then continue the charge processing asynchronously (N1:4:3fd7a028, N3:4:b6bf9b3c). Set a short request timeout (5‑10 s) so the sender will retry on timeout, relying on the idempotency checks to avoid double‑charging (N1:5:99392cbd, N3:5:220c1a3b). Show the user a pending status and update the UI once the async processing completes (N1:6:d8a420f0). Implement exponential backoff for provider retries and route permanently failing events to a dead‑letter queue for manual review (N1:7:8f632d80). This combination of unique keys, database constraints, transactional processing, immediate acknowledgment, and sensible timeouts ensures no double‑charges while keeping the system responsive.
